title: Complexity of Scott Sentences
overall_summary: The paper proves effective versions of several results connecting the infinitary complexity of Scott sentences with definability of automorphism orbits of tuples. Its central technical tool is a Henkin-style consistency-property argument: from a low-complexity Scott sentence, one builds complexity-bounded finite diagrams, shows that a certain extra requirement must fail, and extracts a low-complexity formula generating the relevant type; a back-and-forth argument then turns this into orbit definability. The most directly useful result for the original problem is Theorem 2.6: a computable Πα Scott sentence implies that every tuple orbit is definable by a computable Σ<α infinitary formula. The paper also gives criteria for finitely generated groups to have d-Σ2 or computable d-Σ2 Scott sentences in terms of Π1-definability of generating-tuple orbits, and it identifies self-reflection as exactly the obstruction to such Π1 orbit definitions. These results are especially relevant when automorphisms are determined by finite tuples, because orbit definability of tuples can often be converted into definability of automorphism graphs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 (Scott Isomorphism Theorem, quoted background)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure for a countable language L. Then there is an Lω1ω sentence whose countable models are exactly the structures isomorphic to A. Such a sentence is called a Scott sentence for A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes Scott's theorem as background and does not prove it. It uses the theorem to motivate measuring the internal complexity of a countable structure by the least complexity of one of its Scott sentences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 (Vaught, quoted background)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a countable language, and let Mod(L) be the Polish space of L-structures with universe ω, with the topology generated by finitary quantifier-free L∪C sentences where C is a countable set of constants naming the natural numbers. If K ⊆ Mod(L) is closed under isomorphism, then K is Πα in the Borel hierarchy if and only if there is a Πα sentence φ of Lω1ω such that K = Mod(φ). The paper notes that the analogous statements for Σα and d-Σα follow as corollaries.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted as background. The role in the paper is to relate the syntactic complexity of infinitary sentences to the descriptive-set-theoretic Borel complexity of invariant classes of countable structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3 (Vanden Boom, quoted background)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a computable language, and let K ⊆ Mod(L) be closed under isomorphism. For a computable ordinal α, K is effective Πα in the effective Borel hierarchy if and only if there is a computable Πα sentence φ of Lω1ω such that K = Mod(φ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This effective analogue of Vaught's theorem is quoted as background. The paper uses the general philosophy that computable infinitary sentence complexity corresponds to effective Borel complexity of invariant classes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1 (Montalbán)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α ≥ 1 be a countable ordinal, and let A be a countable structure for a countable language L. Then A has a Πα+1 Scott sentence if and only if, for every finite tuple ā from A, the automorphism orbit of ā is definable in A by a Σα formula of Lω1ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the direction from orbit definitions to a Scott sentence, the paper gives the standard Scott construction. For each tuple ā choose a Σα formula φ_ā(x̄) defining its automorphism orbit. For the empty tuple, include the sentence ρ_empty asserting both that every one-element orbit is nonempty, ∧_b ∃x φ_b(x), and that every element lies in some one-element orbit, ∀x ∨_b φ_b(x). For each tuple ā, include a sentence ρ_ā saying that whenever ū is in the orbit of ā, then every one-point extension orbit over ā is realized over ū and every element over ū lies in one of those extension orbits: ∀ū[φ_ā(ū) → (∧_b ∃x φ_{ā,b}(ū,x) and ∀x ∨_b φ_{ā,b}(ū,x))]. The conjunction of all these sentences is Πα+1 and is a Scott sentence by the usual back-and-forth argument. The converse is Montalbán's theorem; the paper later adapts its proof to obtain Theorem 2.2 and its effective version.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3 (building a chain from a consistency property)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a countable language and C a countably infinite set of new constants. A consistency property is a nonempty family 𝒞 of finite sets S of Lω1ω(L∪C)-sentences in normal form, closed under the following Henkin-style requirements: universal-conjunctive formulas in S can be instantiated; existential-disjunctive formulas in S can be witnessed; each finitary quantifier-free formula or its negation can be decided; function symbols receive values among constants; distinct constants are not forced equal; and the finitary quantifier-free part of S is consistent. If 𝒞 is such a consistency property, then there is a countable increasing chain S0 ⊆ S1 ⊆ ... of members of 𝒞 such that the set {Sn : n ∈ ω} itself satisfies the same consistency-property requirements.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a standard Henkin construction. The finite sets S ∈ 𝒞 have only finitely many immediate extension requirements of the kinds listed in the definition, and there are only countably many possible requirements overall, because the language and constant set are countable. Enumerate the requirements and, stage by stage, use the relevant consistency-property clause to extend the current finite set inside 𝒞 so as to meet the next requirement. The resulting chain meets all requirements cofinally, so the chain itself functions as a consistency property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4 (a consistency-property chain yields a model)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Sn)n∈ω be an increasing chain of finite sets of Lω1ω(L∪C)-sentences such that {Sn : n ∈ ω} is a consistency property in the sense of Lemma 2.3. Then the atomic and negated atomic sentences in ⋃n Sn form the atomic diagram of a well-defined L-structure B with universe C. Moreover, every sentence φ ∈ ⋃n Sn is true in B under the interpretation of constants by themselves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each relation symbol R, define RB(c1,...,cn) to hold exactly when R(c1,...,cn) occurs in ⋃n Sn; the decision and finite consistency clauses make this well-defined. For each function symbol F, define F B(c1,...,cn)=d when F(c1,...,cn)=d occurs in ⋃n Sn; the totality, distinctness, and finite consistency clauses ensure existence and uniqueness. Equality between constants is exactly identity of constants. An induction on terms shows that term evaluations agree with the equations in ⋃n Sn. An induction on finitary quantifier-free formulas then gives correctness for those formulas, and a final induction on infinitary formulas, using the Henkin clauses for conjunctions, disjunctions, universal instances, and existential witnesses, shows that every sentence placed in ⋃n Sn is satisfied in B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure for a countable language L, and let α ≥ 2 be a countable ordinal. If A has a Πα Scott sentence, then for every finite tuple ā from A, the automorphism orbit of ā is definable in A by a Σ<α formula, meaning a Σβ formula for some β < α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write a Πα Scott sentence for A as θ = ∧_i ∀ū_i θ_i(ū_i), where each θ_i is a disjunction of existential formulas θ_i(ū_i)=∨_j ∃v̄_{i,j} ψ_{i,j}(ū_i,v̄_{i,j}) with ψ_{i,j} of complexity strictly below α in the appropriate sense. Fix a countable set C of Henkin constants. Define 𝒞 to consist of all finite sets S of Lω1ω(L∪C)-sentences, each of complexity Σβ or Πβ for some β with β+1<α, such that the constants occurring in S can be injectively interpreted in A so as to make all sentences in S true. This 𝒞 satisfies the six consistency-property clauses and an additional witnessing clause for θ: for each i and tuple c̄ of constants, some disjunct j and witness tuple d̄ can be added so that ψ_{i,j}(c̄,d̄) is included. Thus any chain satisfying these clauses yields, by Proposition 2.4, a model of θ. Now fix a tuple ā of distinct elements. Consider an additional requirement saying that, for every finite S ∈ 𝒞 and every tuple c̄ of distinct constants of the same length as ā, there is some Π<α formula ψ(x̄)=∧_i ∀ū_i ψ_i(x̄,ū_i) true of ā and an extension S′ ∈ 𝒞 of S that contains a negated instance neg(ψ_i(c̄,d̄)) for some i,d̄. If this added requirement held, Lemma 2.3 would produce a model of θ in which no tuple realizes all Π<α formulas true of ā, contradicting that θ is a Scott sentence for A. Therefore the added requirement fails. Hence for some S and c̄, no Π<α formula true of ā can be refuted in that way. Let c̄′ be the other constants appearing in S, and let χ(c̄,c̄′) be the finite conjunction of S together with formulas asserting the needed distinctness of these constants. Then A satisfies ∃x̄ χ(ā,x̄), and for every Π<α formula ψ true of ā, A satisfies the Π<α implication ∀ū[(∃x̄ χ(ū,x̄)) → ψ(ū)]. Thus φ_ā(ū):=∃x̄ χ(ū,x̄) is a Σ<α formula which generates the complete Π<α type of ā. Applying Lemma 2.5 to the family of these formulas shows that each φ_ā actually defines the automorphism orbit of ā. Tuples with repeated entries are handled by including their equality pattern.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Theorem 2.2, suppose that for each finite tuple ā from A there is a Σ<α formula φ_ā(x̄) true of ā and implying every Π<α formula true of ā. Let F be the family of finite partial maps sending a tuple ā to a tuple b̄ satisfying φ_ā. Then F has the back-and-forth property. Consequently, φ_ā defines the automorphism orbit of ā.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First show symmetry: if b̄ satisfies φ_ā, then ā satisfies φ_b̄. Indeed, neg(φ_b̄) is Π<α. If neg(φ_b̄) were true of ā, then since b̄ satisfies φ_ā and φ_ā implies all Π<α truths of ā, neg(φ_b̄) would be true of b̄, contradiction. For the back direction, suppose b̄ satisfies φ_ā and fix an element d. If no c made ā,c satisfy φ_{b̄,d}, then the Π<α formula ∀y neg(φ_{b̄,d}(x̄,y)) would be true of ā and hence true of b̄, contradicting that b̄,d satisfies φ_{b̄,d}. So some c exists with A ⊨ φ_{b̄,d}(ā,c). By the symmetry argument applied to the extended tuples, b̄,d satisfies φ_{ā,c}, giving the required back extension. The forth extension is analogous, using that ā satisfies φ_b̄. The back-and-forth system then yields an automorphism taking ā to b̄, so φ_ā defines exactly the orbit of ā.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be the linear order of order type ω^ω. Then all tuple orbits in A are definable by Σ<ω formulas, indeed by natural computable Σ<ω formulas, but A has no Πω Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses two standard facts. First, ω^ω ≤_ω ω^ω+1 in Karp's back-and-forth relation, so every Πω sentence true in ω^ω is true in ω^ω+1. Therefore no Πω sentence can be a Scott sentence for ω^ω. Second, for each β<ω^ω there are computable Σ<ω formulas λ(x) and μ(x,y) saying respectively that the initial interval to the left of x has order type β and that the interval between x and y has order type β. Since ordinals are rigid, the orbit of a tuple is just the tuple itself. If ā=(a1,...,an) and the interval to the left of ai has type β_i, then the conjunction of the corresponding λ_i(x_i) formulas defines ā.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A expand the order of type ω^ω by unary predicates U0,U1,U2,..., where U0 names the interval [0,ω) and, for n≥1, Un names the interval [ωn,ωn+1). Then all tuple orbits are definable by computable Σ<ω formulas, and A has a computable Πω Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The orbit definitions are inherited from the interval-defining formulas used in Example 1, now aided by the unary predicates naming the successive blocks. A computable Πω Scott sentence is obtained as the conjunction of: a computable Π2 sentence ∀x ∨_n Un(x) saying every element lies in some block; a finitary Π2 sentence saying that < is a linear ordering of the universe and that all elements of Un precede all elements of Un+1; and computable Σ<ω sentences specifying the exact order type of each predicate-defined block Un.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a computable language, let A be an L-structure, not necessarily with a computable copy, and let α ≥ 2 be a computable ordinal. If A has a computable Πα Scott sentence, then for every finite tuple ā from A, the automorphism orbit of ā is definable in A by a computable Σ<α formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the effective version of Theorem 2.2. Replace arbitrary infinitary formulas by computable infinitary formulas, so infinite conjunctions and disjunctions range over c.e. sets of indices. Define the consistency property 𝒞 to consist of finite sets of computable Σβ or computable Πβ sentences, with β+1<α, that are satisfiable in A by an injective interpretation of the finitely many constants involved. The same Henkin construction works effectively because all relevant syntactic decompositions are computable or c.e. The same failure of the added refutation condition yields, for each tuple ā, a computable Σ<α formula φ_ā true of ā and implying all computable Π<α formulas true of ā. Lemma 2.7, the effective analogue of Lemma 2.5, then gives a back-and-forth system, so φ_ā defines the orbit of ā.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the effective setting of Theorem 2.6, if each tuple ā has a computable Σ<α formula φ_ā true of ā and implying all computable Π<α formulas true of ā, then the family of finite maps taking ā to b̄ with A ⊨ φ_ā(b̄) has the back-and-forth property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is exactly the proof of Lemma 2.5, with all formulas restricted to the computable infinitary hierarchy. Negations of computable Σ<α formulas are computable Π<α formulas, and the universal formula ∀y neg(φ_{b̄,d}(x̄,y)) remains computable Π<α. These closure facts allow the same symmetry and forth/back extension arguments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a computable structure A such that every tuple orbit in A is definable by a computable Σ1 formula, in fact by a finitary quantifier-free formula, but A has no computable Π2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction begins with a computable subtree T of 2^{<ω} with no terminal nodes and exactly one non-isolated path p, where p is noncomputable. The paper describes an explicit construction in which all σ0 extensions are included and the unique non-isolated path encodes the halting set using blocks 0^{s0}1^{k0}0^{s1}1^{k1}..., where k_i enumerate halting inputs and s_i are their halting times. The language has unary predicates Un for n∈ω. Elements of the intended structures represent paths through T; Un(a) holds exactly when the represented path has value 1 at n. A computable first-order theory T* says that every element follows T to each finite level and that every finite node σ∈T is realized by arbitrarily many elements. Countable models of T* have infinitely many elements on each isolated path and may additionally have elements on the unique non-isolated path. Let A be the model with no elements representing the non-isolated path. It has a computable copy by partitioning ω into infinitely many computable infinite sets and assigning blocks to isolated paths of the form σ followed by all zeros. In A, if σ_i isolates the path represented by a_i, then the conjunction of the equality pattern of the tuple and the finite predicate descriptions σ_i(x_i) defines the tuple orbit, because the language is purely unary. Let B be the model with exactly one element b representing the non-isolated path and with A as the substructure of isolated-path elements. To show that A has no computable Π2 Scott sentence, it suffices to prove that every computable Σ2 sentence true in B is true in A. Given such a sentence ∨_i ∃ū_i φ_i(ū_i), with φ_i computable Π1, choose a true instance B ⊨ φ_i(b,ā). Let δ(v̄) be a finitary quantifier-free formula generating the type of ā, and let p(x,v̄) be the c.e. set of finitary universal conjuncts of φ_i(x,v̄). Form Γ = T* plus δ(c̄) plus p(d,c̄). If Γ proved σ(d) for every finite initial segment σ of the noncomputable path p, then p would be computable. Hence for some σ⊆p, Γ∪{¬σ(d)} has a model C in which d and c̄ represent isolated paths. The isolated-path substructure of C is isomorphic to A and contains d,c̄, so A realizes the same Π1 instance φ_i(a′,ā). Thus A satisfies the original Σ2 sentence. Since B is not isomorphic to A but satisfies every computable Π2 sentence true of A, no computable Π2 Scott sentence for A exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α ≥ 1 be a computable ordinal. Suppose A is a computable structure and A has a parameter-free Σα Scott family Φ consisting of computable Σα formulas; in the paper's usage, Φ is a family of formulas such that every tuple of A satisfies some formula in Φ and formulas in Φ isolate automorphism orbits. Then A has a computable Πα+1 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key claim is that there is a computable procedure which, given a tuple c̄ from A, outputs a computable Σα formula φ_c̄(x̄) defining the orbit of c̄. Fix a notation a for α and assume formulas in Φ have indices of the form (Σ,a,e). Let R be the Σα relation of pairs (c̄,e) such that the formula ψ_{c̄,e} with index (Σ,a,e) lies in Φ and is true of c̄. Uniformly construct computable Σα formulas τ_{c̄,e}, built from tautology and contradiction, such that τ_{c̄,e} is equivalent to truth if (c̄,e)∈R and to falsehood otherwise. Then define φ_c̄(x̄) as the disjunction over e of τ_{c̄,e} ∧ ψ_{c̄,e}(x̄). This is a computable Σα orbit formula for c̄. Using these uniformly produced orbit formulas, build a Scott sentence in the usual way: include φ_empty and, for every tuple ā, include the computable Πα+1 extension sentence ∀x̄[φ_ā(x̄) → ((∀y)∨_b φ_{ā,b}(x̄,y) and ∧_b ∃y φ_{ā,b}(x̄,y))]. The conjunction is a computable Πα+1 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1 (A. Miller, quoted with sketch)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable ordinal α ≥ 1, if a countable structure A has both a Πα+1 Scott sentence and a Σα+1 Scott sentence, then A has a d-Σα Scott sentence, i.e. a Scott sentence that is the conjunction of a Σα sentence and a Πα sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper sketches A. Miller's argument via D. Miller's separation theorem. D. Miller's result says that if two disjoint invariant classes A and B in Mod(L) are both axiomatized by Πα+1 sentences, then there is a separator containing A and disjoint from B which is a countable union of classes axiomatized by d-Σα sentences. Apply this with A = Mod(φ), where φ is the Πα+1 Scott sentence for the structure, and B = Mod(neg(ψ)), where ψ is the Σα+1 Scott sentence and neg(ψ) is Πα+1. Since Mod(neg(ψ)) is the complement of Mod(φ), the separator must be exactly Mod(φ). The corresponding sentence is a countable disjunction of d-Σα sentences; one disjunct is true in A, and because the union equals the isomorphism class of A, that true disjunct is itself a d-Σα Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α ≥ 2 be a computable ordinal. If a structure A has both a computable Πα Scott sentence and a computable Σα Scott sentence, then A has a computable d-Σ<α Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If α is a limit ordinal, a computable Σα sentence is a c.e. disjunction of lower-complexity pieces; since A satisfies one disjunct, there is already a computable Σ<α Scott sentence, yielding the desired lower bound. For successor α=β+1, the result is Lemma 3.3: from computable Πβ+1 and Σβ+1 Scott sentences one extracts a computable d-Σβ Scott sentence. Combining these cases gives the stated computable d-Σ<α conclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3 (Main Lemma)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α ≥ 1 be a computable ordinal. If a structure A has a computable Σα+1 Scott sentence and a computable Πα+1 Scott sentence, then A has a computable d-Σα Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the computable Σα+1 Scott sentence as φ = ∨_{i∈W} ∃ū_i φ_i(ū_i), where W is c.e. and each φ_i is computable Πα. Choose i and a tuple ā in A such that A ⊨ φ_i(ā). Since A also has a computable Πα+1 Scott sentence, Theorem 2.6 gives a computable Σα formula γ(ū) defining the automorphism orbit of ā in A. Now form the conjunction of the computable Σα sentence ∃ū γ(ū) and the sentence ∀ū(γ(ū) → φ_i(ū)), which is logically equivalent to a computable Πα sentence because γ is computable Σα and φ_i is computable Πα. A satisfies this conjunction. Any countable model satisfying it has some tuple satisfying γ and hence, by the implication, satisfying φ_i, so it satisfies the original Σα+1 Scott sentence φ and is therefore isomorphic to A. Thus the conjunction is a computable d-Σα Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a computable language, let α ≥ 2 be a computable ordinal, and let 𝒜 and ℬ be disjoint subsets of Mod(L), each axiomatized by a computable Πα sentence. Then there is a separator S with 𝒜 ⊆ S and S∩ℬ=∅ such that S is the union of a countable family of classes, each axiomatized by a computable d-Σ<α sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let 𝒜=Mod(φ) and ℬ=Mod(ψ), where φ and ψ are computable Πα. Put φ in the form ∧_{i∈W} ∀ū_i φ_i(ū_i), with W c.e., and φ_i(ū_i)=∨_{j∈W_{e_i}} ∃v̄_{i,j} ξ_{i,j}(ū_i,v̄_{i,j}), where each ξ_{i,j} is Πβ for some β with β+1<α and the indices e_i are computable from i. For each A∈𝒜, define a consistency property 𝒞_A consisting of finite sets of computable Σβ or Πβ sentences, β+1<α, satisfiable in A under an injective assignment of constants. This property also satisfies a φ-witnessing condition: for every S∈𝒞_A, every i∈W, and every tuple c̄ of constants, one can extend S by some ξ_{i,j}(c̄,d̄). Any chain satisfying this condition yields a model of φ. Write ψ similarly with subformulas θ_{i,j}. Because 𝒜 and ℬ are disjoint, 𝒞_A cannot also satisfy the analogous ψ-witnessing condition; otherwise a Henkin chain would yield a model of both φ and ψ. Therefore, for each A∈𝒜, there are S∈𝒞_A and a tuple c̄ for some ψ-index i such that no extension by any θ_{i,j}(c̄,d̄) is satisfiable in A. Let c̄′ be the other constants in S, and let χ(c̄,c̄′) be the conjunction of S plus the relevant distinctness assertions. If c̄ repeats constants, define ρ(ū_i,x̄) by replacing constants with variables consistently and adding equality and inequality formulas matching the pattern of c̄. Then A satisfies both ∃ū_i x̄ ρ(ū_i,x̄), a computable Σ<α sentence, and the computable Π<α sentence ∀ū_i[((∃x̄)ρ(ū_i,x̄)) → ∧_{j∈W_{e_i}} ∀v̄_{i,j} neg(θ_{i,j}(ū_i,v̄_{i,j}))]. No model of ψ can satisfy both, since a model of ψ would have to realize some θ_{i,j} for every ū_i. Their conjunction is a computable d-Σ<α sentence true in A and false in every member of ℬ. Taking the union over all A∈𝒜 gives a separator; there are only countably many possible pairs of computable infinitary sentences, so this separator is a countable union of such d-Σ<α classes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every finitely generated group G has a Σ3 Scott sentence. More explicitly, if ā is a finite generating tuple for G and ⟨x̄⟩≅⟨ā⟩ denotes the Π1 formula saying that x̄ satisfies exactly the same group relators and non-relators as ā, then a Σ3 Scott sentence is ∃x̄[⟨x̄⟩≅⟨ā⟩ and ∀y ∨_w w(x̄)=y], where w ranges over group words.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula ⟨x̄⟩≅⟨ā⟩ states the full atomic diagram of the subgroup generated by x̄ in terms of which words are equal or unequal to the identity, matching the corresponding facts about ā. The conjunct ∀y ∨_w w(x̄)=y says that x̄ generates the whole group. In any countable group satisfying the displayed sentence, the tuple x̄ has the same presentation by generators and relators as ā and generates the whole model, so the map sending ā to x̄ extends to an isomorphism from G onto the model. The displayed sentence has complexity Σ3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finitely generated group G, the following are equivalent: (1) G has a Π3 Scott sentence; (2) G has a d-Σ2 Scott sentence; (3) for some finite generating tuple ā of G, the automorphism orbit of ā is definable by a Π1 formula; (4) for every finite generating tuple ā of G, the automorphism orbit of ā is definable by a Π1 formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication (2)⇒(1) is immediate because a d-Σ2 sentence can be viewed at Π3 complexity. Since Proposition 4.1 gives a Σ3 Scott sentence for every finitely generated group, A. Miller's theorem gives (1)⇒(2). For (1)⇒(4), assume G has a Π3 Scott sentence and let ā be any generating tuple. By Montalbán's theorem, the orbit of ā is defined by a Σ2 formula φ(x̄)=∨_i ∃ū_i φ_i(x̄,ū_i), where each φ_i is Π1. Choose a true disjunct and witnesses b̄ with G ⊨ φ_i(ā,b̄). Since ā generates G, there is a tuple of words w̄ such that b̄=w̄(ā). Then φ_i(x̄,w̄(x̄)) is a Π1 formula defining the orbit of ā. The implication (4)⇒(3) is trivial. For (3)⇒(1), let ā be a generating tuple whose orbit is defined by a Π1 formula ψ(ū). Every tuple b̄ in G is equal to w̄(ā) for some tuple of words w̄, and its orbit is then defined by the Σ2 formula ∃ū(ψ(ū) and x̄=w̄(ū)). Thus all tuple orbits are Σ2-definable, so Montalbán's theorem gives a Π3 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3 (Ho's Generating Set Lemma)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a computable finitely generated group. Suppose φ(x̄) is a computable Σ2 formula, satisfied in G, such that every tuple satisfying φ in G generates G. Then G has a computable d-Σ2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a generating tuple ā satisfying φ. Ho's Scott sentence is the conjunction of two parts. The first is the computable Π2 sentence ∀x̄[φ(x̄) → ∀y ∨_w w(x̄)=y], saying that every tuple satisfying φ generates the whole group. The second is the computable Σ2 sentence ∃x̄[φ(x̄) and ⟨x̄⟩≅⟨ā⟩], saying that there is such a tuple with exactly the same relators and non-relators as ā. Any model of the conjunction has a generating tuple with the same group presentation as ā, hence is isomorphic to G. Conversely G satisfies the sentence by choice of ā.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finitely generated group. Suppose there is a Σ2 formula φ(x̄), satisfied in G, such that every tuple satisfying φ in G generates G. Then G has a d-Σ2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the non-effective analogue of Ho's Generating Set Lemma. Use the same two conjuncts as in Lemma 4.3, but without computability requirements: a Π2 sentence saying every φ-tuple generates, and a Σ2 sentence saying some φ-tuple has the same relators and non-relators as a chosen generating tuple of G. Their conjunction is d-Σ2 and characterizes G up to isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a computable finitely generated group G, the following are equivalent: (1) G has a computable Π3 Scott sentence; (2) G has a computable d-Σ2 Scott sentence; (3) for some finite generating tuple ā of G, the automorphism orbit of ā is definable by a computable Π1 formula; (4) for every finite generating tuple ā of G, the automorphism orbit of ā is definable by a computable Π1 formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication (2)⇒(1) is immediate. For (1)⇒(4), assume G has a computable Π3 Scott sentence and let ā be a generating tuple. By Theorem 2.6, the orbit of ā is defined by a computable Σ2 formula φ(x̄)=∨_{i∈W} ∃ū_i φ_i(x̄,ū_i), where W is c.e. and each φ_i is computable Π1. Pick i∈W and witnesses b̄ with G ⊨ φ_i(ā,b̄). Since ā generates G, b̄=w̄(ā) for some tuple of words w̄. Then the formula φ_i(x̄,w̄(x̄)) is computable Π1 and defines the orbit of ā. The implication (4)⇒(3) is immediate. For (3)⇒(2), let ā be a generating tuple whose orbit is defined by a computable Π1 formula; this formula is also a computable Σ2 formula, and all tuples in that orbit generate G. Ho's Generating Set Lemma then gives a computable d-Σ2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finitely generated group G, G is self-reflective if and only if there is a finite generating tuple ā whose automorphism orbit is not definable by any Π1 formula. Here G is self-reflective, in the sense of Harrison-Trainor and Ho, if there are a generating tuple ā of G and a tuple b̄ generating a proper subgroup H of G such that (G,ā) is isomorphic to (H,b̄), and every existential formula true of b̄ in G is true of b̄ in H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction, suppose self-reflection is witnessed by ā,b̄,H. Since every existential formula true of b̄ in G is true of b̄ in H, and (H,b̄)≅(G,ā), those existential facts also hold of ā in G. Equivalently, every universal formula true of ā in G is true of b̄ in G. Thus every Π1 formula true of ā is also true of b̄. But b̄ generates a proper subgroup, so it is not in the automorphism orbit of the generating tuple ā. Hence no Π1 formula can define that orbit. Conversely, suppose ā generates G and its orbit is not Π1-definable. Let ψ(x̄) be the Π1 formula which is the conjunction of all universal formulas true of ā. Since ψ does not define the orbit, there is b̄ satisfying ψ but not in the orbit of ā. If b̄ generated G, then matching the universal relators and non-relators of ā would make b̄ another generating tuple inducing an automorphism, contradiction; hence b̄ generates a proper subgroup H. The tuple b̄ has the same group presentation as ā, so (G,ā)≅(H,b̄). Also, if an existential formula is true of b̄ in G, then its negation, a universal formula, is not true of b̄; because b̄ satisfies all universal formulas true of ā, that existential formula must be true of ā in G, and hence true of b̄ in H under the isomorphism. Thus G is self-reflective.

proof_techniques.name: Henkin consistency properties with complexity bounds
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses a Henkin-style consistency property: finite sets of infinitary sentences over a countable set of new constants are required to be extendable by universal instances, existential witnesses, quantifier-free decisions, and function values, while preserving finite quantifier-free consistency. A chain meeting all these requirements yields a genuine countable structure whose universe is the set of constants, and every sentence placed into the chain is true in the resulting structure. In Theorem 2.2 and Theorem 2.6, the finite sets are restricted to formulas of complexity Σβ or Πβ with β+1<α, so the Henkin construction preserves the desired complexity bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a general way to turn failure of a desired low-complexity definition into a new isomorphic copy with controlled properties. In the original problem, a similar consistency-property construction could be used to test whether finite parameters force low-complexity definitions of automorphism graphs, or to build a copy/automorphism witnessing non-definability.

proof_techniques.name: Failure of an added Henkin requirement yields a type generator
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 2.2, after setting up a consistency property that builds models of a Πα Scott sentence, the authors add a hypothetical requirement saying that every tuple of constants can be made to fail some Π<α formula true of a fixed tuple ā. If that requirement held, the Henkin construction would produce a model of the Scott sentence with no tuple realizing the complete Π<α type of ā, impossible because all models of a Scott sentence are isomorphic to A. Therefore the requirement fails at some finite set S and constants c̄; the finite conjunction χ of S then gives a Σ<α formula ∃x̄ χ(ū,x̄) which implies every Π<α formula true of ā.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This extraction mechanism can be adapted whenever a Scott-sentence-like uniqueness condition prevents omitting a type. For automorphism definability, one can try to apply the same idea to tuples such as (parameters, x, image-parameters, y), then convert the resulting type generator into a definition of the graph of an automorphism when uniqueness is available.

proof_techniques.name: Back-and-forth from generated dual types
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.5 shows that if each tuple ā has a Σ<α formula φ_ā which implies every Π<α formula true of ā, then the maps ā↦b̄ with b̄ satisfying φ_ā automatically form a back-and-forth system. The key observations are that negations of the φ formulas lie in Π<α and that formulas of the form ∀y neg(φ_{b̄,d}(x̄,y)) also lie in Π<α. These closure facts force symmetry and both extension directions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful whenever one can define a candidate relation between finite tuples but still needs to prove that the relation is exactly orbit equivalence. For the original problem, it suggests a route from low-complexity tuple-type information to actual automorphisms, which is essential if a finite base or finite generating tuple is present.

proof_techniques.name: Effectiveization by computable infinitary syntax
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.6 repeats the non-effective argument of Theorem 2.2 with computable infinitary formulas: infinite disjunctions and conjunctions are over c.e. sets, and all syntactic decompositions are effective. The same consistency-property and back-and-forth proof goes through, producing computable Σ<α orbit definitions from a computable Πα Scott sentence, even when the structure itself is not assumed to have a computable copy.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is explicitly computability-theoretic and allows relativization to a cone. This technique should relativize to an oracle C or to a presentation B⊕C, giving a way to obtain oracle-computable infinitary orbit definitions from oracle-computable Scott-sentence information.

proof_techniques.name: Selecting a true disjunct of a Σ Scott sentence
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.3 proves an effective Miller-style result by taking a computable Σα+1 Scott sentence, choosing a true disjunct ∃ū φ_i(ū) in A, and then using a computable Πα+1 Scott sentence to obtain a computable Σα formula γ defining the orbit of the witnessing tuple. The conjunction ∃ū γ(ū) and ∀ū(γ(ū)→φ_i(ū)) is then a computable d-Σα Scott sentence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate structure has both positive and negative Scott complexity bounds, this method can lower the complexity and thereby force stronger tuple-orbit definitions. Such lowering may obstruct attempts to make all automorphism graphs non-Σ1-definable from finite parameter data.

proof_techniques.name: Effective invariant separation via failed simultaneous Henkin witnesses
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.4 gives an effective separator for two disjoint computable Πα invariant classes. For a model A of the first class, its bounded-complexity consistency property can witness the first Πα sentence but cannot witness the second, or else a Henkin chain would produce a model of both. The specific finite obstruction to witnessing the second sentence is converted into a computable d-Σ<α sentence true of A and false in every model of the second class; a countable union of these separates the classes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can be useful if the original problem is reformulated as separating structures or expansions with a desired automorphism property from those without it. The proof gives a template for extracting explicit low-complexity invariants from incompatibility of two infinitary theories.

proof_techniques.name: Word substitution lowers orbit complexity in finitely generated groups
proof_techniques.description: In Theorems 4.2 and 4.5, a Σ2 definition of the orbit of a generating tuple ā is reduced to a Π1 definition. If the Σ2 formula is ∨_i ∃ū_i φ_i(x̄,ū_i) and φ_i(ā,b̄) holds, then b̄ is a tuple of words w̄(ā) because ā generates the group. Substituting w̄(x̄) for ū_i yields the Π1 formula φ_i(x̄,w̄(x̄)) defining the same orbit.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a strong warning that finite generation or finite bases can collapse automorphism information to very low definability. In particular, when automorphisms are determined by images of a finite generating tuple, the graph of an automorphism is often expressible by an infinitary disjunction over words or terms using the tuple and its image as parameters.

proof_techniques.name: Generating Set Lemma for d-Σ2 Scott sentences
proof_techniques.description: Ho's Generating Set Lemma says that if a computable Σ2 formula φ picks out only generating tuples in a computable finitely generated group and is satisfied by one tuple with the right relators and non-relators, then the conjunction of a Π2 sentence saying all φ-tuples generate and a Σ2 sentence saying some φ-tuple has the original relator diagram is a computable d-Σ2 Scott sentence. The non-effective analogue is Lemma 4.4.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The lemma isolates the exact use of finite generation in producing low Scott complexity. For the original problem, any proposed example with a finite tuple from which all elements are uniformly term-generated may automatically give low-complexity definitions of automorphisms once that tuple and its image are named.

proof_techniques.name: Self-reflection as obstruction to Π1 orbit definability
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.6 identifies self-reflective finitely generated groups as exactly those having a generating tuple whose orbit is not Π1-definable. The obstruction is the existence of a tuple b̄ generating a proper subgroup H that looks existentially the same in G as it does in H and such that (G,ā)≅(H,b̄). This ensures that every universal, hence every Π1, fact true of ā is also true of b̄, although b̄ is not in the orbit of ā.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a concrete model-theoretic pattern for defeating low-complexity orbit definitions while retaining finite generation. Variants of self-reflection may be useful in constructing structures where finite parameter information cannot define certain automorphisms, though finite generation itself may also make automorphism graphs definable once generators are named.

proof_techniques.name: Unary-predicate tree construction separating orbit definitions from computable Scott complexity
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.8 uses a computable binary tree with a unique noncomputable non-isolated path to build a computable unary-predicate structure whose tuple orbits are quantifier-free definable, yet no computable Π2 Scott sentence exists. The noncomputable path is used to build a nonisomorphic model B that satisfies every computable Π2 sentence true of A, by showing that any computable Σ2 fact true using the nonisolated-path element can be approximated inside A on an isolated path.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This shows that even extremely low orbit definability in one presentation does not by itself guarantee low effective Scott complexity. It may be useful for decoupling automorphism/orbit definability phenomena from computable Scott-sentence bounds when designing or excluding examples on a cone.

other_useful_info: The paper reports that Harrison-Trainor and Ho constructed a computable finitely generated self-reflective group; for any such group the index set is m-complete Σ0_3, and by relativization there is no X-computable d-Σ2 Scott sentence for any oracle X, hence no d-Σ2 Scott sentence at all. The paper lists open problems that may be relevant context: whether there is a finitely presented self-reflective group; whether most finitely generated groups, in a limiting-density sense, are not self-reflective and hence have d-Σ2 Scott sentences; whether there is a computable finitely generated group with a d-Σ2 Scott sentence but no computable d-Σ2 Scott sentence; and how to characterize computable structures with computable Πα+1 Scott sentences. Example 1 uses the standard facts that ω^ω ≤_ω ω^ω+1 in Karp's back-and-forth relation and that intervals of type β<ω^ω are definable by computable Σ<ω formulas. The main external results used are Montalbán's orbit/Scott theorem, A. Miller's d-Σ reduction theorem, D. Miller's invariant separation theorem, and Ho's Generating Set Lemma.
why_relevant: Gives effective versions of results connecting Scott sentence complexity with definability of tuple orbits. Its results about finitely generated structures and definability of generating-tuple orbits are useful because countable automorphism groups often reduce automorphisms to finite bases.
